title: Referee turn-gate trusts a cursor a prior caller set via client-reported nextActiveIndex
evidence: submit_action gates with activeIndex = room.active_player_index (line 137) and authorizeAction requires players[activeIndex].id === actingId. But active_player_index is written by submit_room_action from p_next_index, which comes from nextCursor({ reported: nextActiveIndex }) — a value the PREVIOUS (turn-ending) submitter supplied in its request body (NetworkClient sends body.nextActiveIndex, line 718). nextCursor only rejects reported===activeIndex (non-round-over) and out-of-range; any other in-bounds seat is honored. So the seat that just fired can point the referee cursor at an arbitrary other seat.
impact: A malicious room member who holds the active turn can set the referee's next-turn cursor to any seat (skip a player, hand the turn to a colluder), so the referee will ACCEPT the next action from a seat that is not the engine-correct next player. Canonical state is replay-derived (every honest client recomputes the real successor), so this corrupts the referee's accept-decision / turn ordering at the DB layer, not the rendered game — but it is a turn-gating bypass at the trust boundary (the referee's only authz check rests on attacker-influenced state). Blast radius is limited to the attacker's own room.
recommendation: Do not let the gate's source of truth be a value a peer reported. Either derive the next active seat from the action log + roster server-side, or only trust nextActiveIndex from the seat whose action just advanced the cursor AND verify the reported successor is the deterministic modulo/elimination successor rather than an arbitrary seat.
acceptance_criteria: A member submitting a turn-ending action cannot cause the referee to accept the next action from a seat other than the engine-deterministic successor
Turn-gating decision in authorizeAction no longer depends solely on a peer-reported cursor

title: finish_game persists a client-asserted winner/scoreboard with no log verification
evidence: finish_game checks only that the caller is a room member (line 85) and that winnerId maps to a real seat index (lines 89-94); it then writes rooms.winner and the match_scores row directly from the request body. The scoreboard is bound-checked for shape/range (sanitizeScoreboard) but NOT cross-checked against the action log — roundWins/kills/totalDamage and the winner are whatever the caller sends.
impact: Any member of an active room can declare an arbitrary winner (any seat) and arbitrary final standings, persisted to rooms.winner + match_scores (the durable match record, public-readable). It cannot be undone after the room flips to finished/the row is upserted. Impact is record falsification scoped to the caller's own room; no cross-room effect. Acceptable per ADR 0006's trust-client posture but worth recording as a forgeable durable record.
recommendation: Treat match_scores/winner as advisory, or have the referee re-derive the winner/standings from the room_actions log instead of trusting the client payload. At minimum document that these records are client-asserted and unverifiable.
acceptance_criteria: The persisted winner is verifiable against the action log, OR the record is explicitly documented as unverified client input

title: Player color is accepted with no format or length bound across all write paths
evidence: All three write paths validate color only as `typeof color === 'string' && color.trim().length === 0` — no max length, no CSS/hex format check (contrast playerName, capped at 20). The value is stored in the rooms.players JSONB and broadcast via Realtime to every peer, then assigned to dot.style.background = p.color (Lobby.ts:1259) and used as a canvas fillStyle.
impact: A member can persist an arbitrarily large color string into the shared room row, broadcast to all peers each render — unbounded payload bloat / Realtime amplification in a shared room. NOT XSS: the sink is a CSSOM style property assignment (and canvas fillStyle), which silently ignores invalid values rather than executing markup; player names reach the DOM only via textContent / an escaping helper (HUD.ts buildScoreboard).
recommendation: Validate color against an allowlist/format (hex or named-color regex) and cap its length, in the same shared validator the name uses.
acceptance_criteria: color is rejected with 400 when it exceeds a small max length or fails a format check
Same bound enforced in create_room, join_room, and update_player

title: ROUND_OVER buy regime is gated only by a client-asserted roundOver flag
evidence: isRoundOver = (roundOver === true) is taken straight from the request body (index.ts:136). When true, authorizeAction Regime 2 drops the turn gate entirely and lets any member buy for their OWN seat (tankId must equal p{seatIndex+1}) regardless of whose turn it is or whether the game is actually in ROUND_OVER. The referee runs no engine, so it cannot tell a real round boundary from a forged flag.
impact: A member can insert buy action rows for their own seat at any time by asserting roundOver:true outside an actual between-rounds shop phase, bypassing the normal turn gate for buys. Per the in-code contract the canonical state is the replayed log, so honest engines route/ignore an out-of-phase buy deterministically — game outcome is unaffected; the effect is referee-accepted out-of-phase rows (log bloat / cursor-neutral noise) scoped to the attacker's own room.
recommendation: Have the referee derive the round phase from the action log rather than trusting a body flag, or constrain buy rows to the seat that is active / verified in a shop phase.
acceptance_criteria: The ROUND_OVER buy gate no longer depends solely on a client-supplied roundOver flag
